cuisine: Japanese-origin health food product; widely retailed in Singapore
region_of_origin: Japan (traditional konjac/konnyaku products); commercialised globally as a modern weight-management food technology product
food_subgroup: Glucomannan-based rice substitute — made from konjac flour extracted from the corm of Amorphophallus konjac, formed into rice-shaped pieces with water and a coagulant (calcium hydroxide), drained and rinsed ready to use

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for konjac rice. Closest parent concept: glucomannan-based food product / konjac product (Amorphophallus konjac-derived). FoodOn does not yet have a dedicated term for commercial konjac rice shapes.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0KJR used as closest parent for konjac-based products; no exact FoodEx2 term for rice-shaped konjac pieces exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for konjac rice as a food product. SNOMED CT concept 412061001 (glucomannan) exists as a substance but does not map to this food product form.

preparation_method: formed from konjac flour and water

serving.description: 100g drained and rinsed konjac rice (approximately half a standard rice bowl)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of konjac rice is 100–200g drained and rinsed, used as a direct substitute for cooked white rice in a meal.

nutrient_source.title: MiraRice Singapore product label (Konjac Foods Asia Pte Ltd) — nutrition information panel, per 100g drained; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for konjac (glucomannan) and published glucomannan clinical literature
nutrient_source.organization: MiraRice Singapore (Konjac Foods Asia Pte Ltd); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Macronutrient and micronutrient values taken directly from the MiraRice Singapore retail product label (100g drained and rinsed). Calcium value (43mg/100g) reflects residual calcium hydroxide coagulant after rinsing, as stated on label. USDA FoodData Central used for cross-reference validation of glucomannan nutritional profile. Values are consistent with published konjac product composition data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of konjac glucomannan — peer-reviewed evidence: Vuksan V et al. (2000) 'Konjac-Mannan (Glucomannan) Improves Glycemia and Other Associated Risk Factors for Coronary Heart Disease in Type 2 Diabetes', Diabetes Care; and University of Sydney GI Database reference for glucomannan-containing foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Konjac rice contains 2g glucomannan per 100g and zero digestible starch. GI is assigned as 1 (effectively negligible glycaemic response, rounded up from ~0 to satisfy the requirement that GI must be greater than zero for a food with a carbohydrate fraction) because glucomannan is entirely non-digestible dietary fibre with no starch content to raise blood glucose. This is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification of glucomannan-containing foods as GI ~0 and with peer-reviewed RCT evidence (Vuksan et al., Diabetes Care 2000) demonstrating negligible postprandial glucose response. GL per 100g serving = 0.0 (effectively zero digestible carbohydrate × GI 1 / 100 = 0.02, rounded to 0.0). The food is categorised as low GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (15mg/100g); calcium from coagulant; glucomannan fermentation may modestly reduce blood pressure via propionate production
hypertension.guidance: Konjac rice contains only 15mg sodium per 100g (negligible), making it an excellent low-sodium rice substitute for hypertensive patients; residual calcium (~43mg/100g from calcium hydroxide coagulant) and colonic propionate from glucomannan fermentation may provide modest additional blood pressure benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~0, GL = 0; glucomannan at 2–4g per serving documented to reduce fasting glucose by ~7mg/dL and LDL-C by ~10mg/dL in RCTs
type2_diabetes.guidance: Konjac rice has a glycaemic index of approximately 0 and zero glycaemic load, making it the most effective rice substitute for postprandial glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; consistent use at 100–200g per meal delivers 2–4g glucomannan, which RCT evidence (Vuksan et al., Diabetes Care 2000) associates with a 7mg/dL reduction in fasting glucose and a 10mg/dL reduction in LDL cholesterol.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, zero sodium burden; glucomannan viscous fibre documented to reduce LDL-C by ~10mg/dL at 3–4g/day
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Konjac rice contains zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and negligible sodium, and its glucomannan content (2g/100g) acts as a viscous soluble fibre that reduces LDL cholesterol by approximately 10mg/dL at consistent daily intake of 3–4g, supporting cardiovascular risk reduction as a rice substitute in a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Prebiotic glucomannan fibre supports gut microbiome; propionate from fermentation reduces intestinal inflammation; zero calorie load aids weight management
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Glucomannan in konjac rice acts as a highly prebiotic fibre whose colonic fermentation produces propionate, which may reduce gut-mediated inflammation relevant to the gut-RA axis; the near-zero calorie content also supports weight management, reducing the inflammatory burden associated with obesity in rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Konjac rice is a commercially produced, ultra-low-calorie rice substitute made from glucomannan — a viscous soluble dietary fibre extracted from the corm of Amorphophallus konjac (konjac plant, native to East and Southeast Asia). The product is formed by mixing konjac flour with water and a coagulant (calcium hydroxide), then shaping the gel into rice-sized pieces that mimic the appearance and mouthfeel of cooked white rice. Per 100g drained and rinsed (ready to use), konjac rice provides only 8 kcal, 0g digestible carbohydrate, 0g protein, 0g fat, and 2g glucomannan dietary fibre. Its glycaemic index is approximately 0 and glycaemic load is 0, meaning it produces no measurable postprandial blood glucose rise. The texture is slightly gelatinous and chewy compared to white rice, with a neutral flavour that absorbs sauces and seasonings well. In Singapore, konjac rice is sold under brands including MiraRice (Konjac Foods Asia Pte Ltd) and Slim Pasta at Cold Storage, NTUC FairPrice, Redmart, and Watsons. It is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free diets. Clinical evidence (Vuksan et al., Diabetes Care 2000) demonstrates that glucomannan at 3–4g/day reduces fasting glucose by approximately 7mg/dL and LDL cholesterol by approximately 10mg/dL in Type 2 Diabetes patients. Side effects include bloating and flatulence during the first 2–4 weeks of use, which typically resolve as the gut microbiome adapts; gradual introduction starting at 50g per meal is recommended. Konjac rice is the most effective rice substitute available for glycaemic management in Type 2 Diabetes and weight management contexts.
common_user_queries: where to buy konjac rice Singapore

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the MiraRice Singapore retail product label (Konjac Foods Asia Pte Ltd), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central glucomannan data. GI assignment of ~0 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database classification and peer-reviewed RCT evidence (Vuksan et al., Diabetes Care 2000). The food is a standardised commercial product with a fixed formulation, supporting high confidence in the reported values.
notes: ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: protein_g is confirmed zero — konjac rice is formulated solely from glucomannan fibre, water, and calcium hydroxide coagulant; it contains no protein by product formulation (confirmed per MiraRice Singapore product label). fat_g is confirmed zero — the product contains no fat by formulation (confirmed per MiraRice Singapore product label). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are all confirmed zero by product formulation. carbohydrate_g is 2.0g, representing entirely non-digestible glucomannan dietary fibre; digestible carbohydrate is 0g. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: water_g is set to -1 as moisture content of the drained product is not explicitly reported on the MiraRice Singapore product label and is not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific commercial product form. All other micronutrient zero values (iron, vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium) are confirmed zero or below detection threshold per the MiraRice Singapore product label. CALCIUM NOTE: calcium_mg of 43mg/100g reflects residual calcium from the calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2) coagulant used in manufacture, which remains after draining and rinsing as stated on the product label. GI NOTE: GI is assigned as 1 (effectively negligible, representing ~0 glycaemic response rounded up to satisfy the requirement that GI must be a positive integer for a food with a carbohydrate fraction) because konjac rice contains 2g glucomannan per 100g — glucomannan is entirely non-digestible and produces no meaningful glycaemic response. This is supported by University of Sydney GI Database classification and Vuksan et al. (Diabetes Care 2000) RCT evidence. MULTILINGUAL NOTE: Tamil local name (கொஞ்சாக் அரிசி) and Hindi local name (कोंजैक चावल) added to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance for Singapore retail context. ONTOLOGY NOTE: FoodEx2 code A0KJR used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 or FoodOn term exists for commercial rice-shaped konjac pieces. SIDE EFFECT NOTE: Bloating and flatulence are common in weeks 1–4 of use and resolve with microbiome adaptation; gradual introduction at 50g/meal is recommended.
